name: Tarocchino di Bologna — The Real 62-Card Bolognese Deck
description:
# Tarocchino di Bologna

## At a glance

Bologna's own shrunken tarot: a **62-card** pack (the 2s–5s removed) for a fast
trick-taking game that has been played continuously for **over 500 years** — one of the
longest-lived card games in Europe. A core witness to the **A-order**, and the deck the
genealogy uses to correct a common error (see below).

## Origin & dating

Emerged in Bologna in the **late 15th / early 16th century**. The 62-card "tarocchino"
("little tarocco") streamlined the pack for play. A celebrated artistic edition is the
copper-engraved deck commissioned c.1660 from **Giuseppe Maria Mitelli** by Count
Filippo Bentivoglio. The modern descendant game, **Ottocento**, still preserves
16th-century features.

## Provenance & evidence

Continuous printed editions, a living game tradition with documented rules, and named
artistic decks (Mitelli). This is one of the best-attested *use* histories of any tarot
family — we know how it was *played*, not just how it looked.

## Game or divination?

**Game** — and an especially clear one, because the playing tradition never lapsed. The
Bolognese context is overwhelmingly recreational.

## The fear question

**No fear.** A civic pastime. Bologna is also, ironically, where Bernardino preached his
1423 anti-card sermon — yet the city's love of the game outlasted the friar by half a
millennium. The pressure was always on **gambling stakes**, never on the cards as
sorcery.

## Counter-voices

The key correction the repo draws from Bologna is **genealogical, not occult**: the
Tarot de Marseille is **not** descended from the Bolognese Tarocchino. They are
**cousins** — Marseille is C-order, Tarocchino is A-order — both children of the common
trionfi root. Popular histories that draw a straight Bologna→Marseille line are wrong.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Precise emergence date of the 62-card reduction is fuzzy ("late 15th/early 16th").
- The Bolognese trump order has local quirks (e.g., the four "Moors"/Papi) that the
  grammar should capture rather than normalise to Marseille.

## Sources & further reading

- "Tarocco Bolognese," Wikipedia; "Tarocchini," Grokipedia.
- "Tarocchi Bolognesi: Ottocento," pagat.com (rules); "Il giuoco dilettevole delli
  tarocchini di Bologna," Princeton Graphic Arts (Mitelli).

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** Printed Bolognese numbering runs only **5–16**; the low and high trumps are unnumbered (so the 0–21 sort order here is positional, not printed numbering). The four equal-rank Papi predate 1725; only the turbaned-**Moor** imagery is the 1725 change (the Montieri affair / Cardinal Ruffo edict), and two of the four Moors are genuinely identical. The Fibbia origin legend (c.1419) is apocryphal. See [research/decks/tarocchino-bologna.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/decks/tarocchino-bologna.md).

sections.Scene: Il Matto is rendered in the double-ended format standard to Bolognese court cards — a jester figure in patchwork multi-colored clothing and a feathered cap occupies both halves of the card, one right-side up and one inverted. The costume of the Fool is vivid and festive, a patchwork of red, blue, and yellow. This fool is not a vagabond with a bundle but a court jester, suggesting the Bolognese association of the Fool with the carnival figure of professional festivity.
sections.Symbol: Double-ended court jester — the Fool as professional entertainer rather than wandering outcast, folly domesticated within the court's own performance.

sections.Scene: Il Bagatto (the Conjurer or Mountebank) appears in the double-ended format with a figure in a low-crowned hat standing at a table. Flanking figures at either end of the card suggest the carnival or street-performance context of the Bolognese Bagatto. Where the Marseille Bateleur is a solitary tradesman behind his paraphernalia, the Bolognese card embeds him in a scene with attendants or audience, consistent with the conjurer as social performer.
sections.Symbol: Double-ended conjurer with table and attendants — the Magician as public showman, skill displayed in the presence of an audience rather than practiced in solitary dexterity.

sections.Iconography: One of four near-identical crowned/turbaned figures.
sections.In the Bolognese deck: THE FOUR PAPI are the signature of the Bolognese deck: four equal, interchangeable cards that replace the Popess, Empress, Emperor and Pope. They rank only among themselves, by order of play.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarocchino was made to play a fast Bolognese trick-taking game, not for divination. The notes here are historical and structural; any reading is a later overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: The first of four Papi (Moors) who replace the standard trumps II through V in the Bolognese deck. This Moor figure wears a jester's pointed cap in bright colors and holds a long lance or staff. Like all four Papi, he is rendered in the double-ended format with his image mirrored at top and bottom. The Papi Mori replaced the Papessa, Empress, Emperor, and Pope to avoid religious and political controversy, substituting secular court figures for sacred ones.
sections.Symbol: First Moor with pointed hat and lance — secular court authority substituted for sacred office, the decorative foreign figure who stands where pope and emperor once stood.

sections.Scene: The second Papa Moro, rendered in double-ended format. This figure wears a simpler or different colored costume than the first, holding a long lance or spear. The four Papi together form a group of interchangeable but visually distinct Moorish figures, each carrying a lance and dressed in colorful Bolognese court style.
sections.Symbol: Second Moor with lance — the second in the quartet of secular substitutes, a figure of exotic otherness used to fill the positions once held by the Church's hierarchy.

The lance or staff remains the consistent attribute across all four Papi.
sections.Symbol: Third Moor, subtly distinguished — the third secular substitute in the set, distinction held lightly within the quartet's common identity.

sections.Scene: L'Amore (Love) shows the conventional Lovers scene — figures beneath a winged Cupid or amorous arrangement — in the Bolognese trump order at position VI. The Bolognese Amore follows the Italian tradition of the Trionfi sequence, placing the triumph of Love early in the hierarchical sequence after the secular substitutes for religious authority.
sections.Symbol: Love at position VI — the triumph of feeling over office, Eros placed immediately after the figures of secular and spiritual power.

sections.Scene: Il Carro (The Chariot) appears at position VII in the Bolognese order, showing the conventional triumph of the vehicle of power — a crowned or helmeted figure riding in a chariot. In the Italian Trionfi tradition the chariot represents the martial triumph, the military procession that Petrarch placed in his allegorical sequence of ascending victories.
sections.Symbol: The Chariot at position VII — the triumph of martial victory, the vehicle that carries the winner of battles in ceremonial procession.

sections.Scene: La Temperanza (Temperance) appears at position VIII in the Bolognese trump sequence — an earlier position than in the Marseille tradition, where Temperance typically appears at XIV. The Bolognese ordering places the temperate virtue of moderation and measured pouring directly after the Chariot's triumph. The card shows the conventional figure of Temperance with two vessels, pouring liquid from one to the other.
sections.Symbol: Temperance at position VIII — moderation placed immediately after martial triumph, the virtue of measured restraint as the necessary sequel to victory.

Justice in the Bolognese tarocchino follows the conventional personification of equity and measure, the allegorical tribunal that sits over the card's triumphal procession.
sections.Symbol: Justice at position VIIII — the weighing of deeds, equity and measure as the fourth cardinal virtue in the Bolognese ordering.

sections.Scene: La Forza (Strength) appears at position X in the Bolognese sequence, showing the conventional figure of Force — a person subduing a lion, typically a woman opening or closing the animal's mouth. The Bolognese Strength follows the standard Italian iconography of this virtue without the dramatic departures seen in some French decks.
sections.Symbol: Strength at position X — the mastery of instinct through patience and courage, the virtue of force expressed as gentle dominance over the beast.

sections.Scene: La Ruota (The Wheel of Fortune) appears at position XI in the Bolognese sequence. The wheel with its ascending and descending figures — the crowned figure at the apex and the fallen ones below — represents Fortune's indifference. In the Bolognese ordering the Wheel sits mid-sequence between Strength and the Old Man, embedded in the cascade of allegorical triumphs.
sections.Symbol: The Wheel of Fortune at position XI — cyclical rise and fall, Fortune's mechanism turning without preference between the crown and the fall.

sections.Iconography: A bent old man with an hourglass — Time, the Bolognese 'Hermit'.
sections.In the Bolognese deck: Time and age.

sections.Scene: Il Vecchio (The Old Man) appears at position XII in the Bolognese sequence, replacing the Hermit (L'Hermite) of the Marseille tradition. Where the Hermit carries a lantern, the Bolognese Old Man is sometimes depicted as Father Time with an hourglass, emphasizing temporality and the passage of years rather than the search for inner light. He represents the triumph of Time over earthly power.
sections.Symbol: The Old Man at position XII — time personified, the triumph of temporality that follows Fortune's wheel, the elder who has outlasted the figures above him.

sections.Scene: Il Traditore (The Traitor) appears at position XIII in the Bolognese sequence, the Bolognese name for the card known in other traditions as the Hanged Man (Il Penduto). The term 'Traitor' emphasizes the judicial nature of the suspension — in Renaissance Italy, traitors were hung by one foot as a mark of shame, making this punishment iconography more explicit in the Bolognese naming.
sections.Symbol: The Traitor at position XIII — treachery punished through enforced suspension, the judicial inversion that names its subject's crime.

sections.Scene: La Morte (Death) appears at position XIIII in the Bolognese sequence, following the Traitor. Death's skeleton with scythe mows the field of the living in the conventional Trionfi tradition. The Bolognese Death card follows the Italian iconographic convention of the skeleton as universal harvester, the triumph of mortality that follows upon betrayal and judgment.
sections.Symbol: Death at position XIIII — the skeleton harvester who follows judgment, the triumph of mortality in the allegorical sequence.

sections.Scene: Il Diavolo (The Devil) appears at position XV in the Bolognese sequence, following Death. The Devil's throne or pedestal with bound figures — the conventional tableau of demonic bondage — represents the triumph of spiritual peril in the allegorical sequence. The Bolognese Devil follows the standard Italian iconography without the wild departures of some northern European decks.
sections.Symbol: The Devil at position XV — the triumph of spiritual bondage, the infernal figure who holds sway after Death has passed.

sections.Iconography: A tower struck by a bolt — called 'La Saetta' (the lightning) in Bologna.
sections.In the Bolognese deck: Sudden ruin.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarocchino was made to play a fast Bolognese trick-taking game, not for divination. The notes here are historical and structural; any reading is a later overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: La Torre (The Tower) appears at position XVI in the Bolognese sequence, showing the conventional lightning-struck tower with figures falling. In the Italian tradition this card is often called 'Il Fulmine' (The Lightning) or 'La Saetta' (The Arrow/Bolt), emphasizing the celestial strike rather than the architectural ruin. The Bolognese Tower follows the standard Italian iconography of catastrophic celestial rupture.
sections.Symbol: The Tower at position XVI — the lightning bolt's catastrophic strike, celestial rupture descending on the structure of presumption.

sections.Iconography: A figure beneath a great star.

sections.Scene: La Stella (The Star) appears at position XVII in the Bolognese sequence. The conventional Star shows a figure pouring water beneath a sky of stars — the Italian Star typically follows the Marseille iconographic type more closely than some French variants. The star card in the tarocchino represents celestial guidance in the sequence that ascends through Moon and Sun toward the World and Angel.
sections.Symbol: The Star at position XVII — celestial light and guidance, the first of the three astral trumps that crown the Bolognese ascending sequence.

sections.Scene: La Luna (The Moon) appears at position XVIII in a format distinctive to the Bolognese tradition. The double-ended card shows two identical female figures standing facing inward toward a large round moon face at center, with stars scattered around. The bilateral symmetry of the two figures flanking the central moon is characteristic of the Bolognese lunar card, entirely different from the Marseille type with dogs, towers, and a pool.
sections.Symbol: Two figures flanking a central moon face — the lunar duality of the Bolognese tradition, the moon as axis between two mirroring presences rather than a solitary celestial emblem.

sections.Scene: Il Sole (The Sun) appears at position XVIIII in the distinctive Bolognese format. Three sun faces with radiating rays appear in the double-ended composition — a central rider or figure on horseback holds a whip or flag between two sun faces, one above and one below. The three-sun arrangement with the central rider is one of the most iconographically distinctive features of the Bolognese tarocchino.
sections.Symbol: Three suns with central rider — the solar triumph in its Bolognese multiplied form, the sun's power emphasized through triplication and the active figure who rides between its faces.

sections.Scene: Il Mondo (The World) appears at position XX in the Bolognese sequence — one position earlier than in standard tarot, where the World is typically the last numbered trump. The double-ended card shows two jester or court figures in colorful costumes standing at either end, flanking a large dark globe or sphere at the center that bears a cross or geometric pattern. This is entirely distinct from the Marseille dancer-in-wreath and represents the world as a globe held between two figures.
sections.Symbol: Two figures flanking a globe at position XX — the world as a sphere held between mirroring presences, completion as a tangible orb rather than a wreath of endless return.

sections.Iconography: Angels with trumpets; the dead rise.
sections.In the Bolognese deck: The HIGHEST trump of the Bolognese deck — uniquely placed ABOVE the World.
sections.Tradition Note: The Tarocchino was made to play a fast Bolognese trick-taking game, not for divination. The notes here are historical and structural; any reading is a later overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** In the C-order the Angel/Judgement sits *below* the World; the Bolognese A-order **raises it to the top** [@web_pollett_orders; @web_tarocco_bolognese_wp].

sections.Scene: L'Angelo (The Angel) appears at position XXI — the highest numbered trump in the Bolognese sequence, where standard tarot places it at XX. The double-ended card shows a winged angel figure in symmetrical format, trumpeting or blessing from the card's center. The Bolognese reversal of Mondo (XX) and Angelo (XXI) places divine judgment above worldly completion, the angel having the final word in the hierarchical ascent.
sections.Symbol: The Angel at position XXI — divine judgment as the highest triumph, the winged messenger who completes the sequence by placing heaven's call above the world.

sections.Scene: The Ace of Coins (Denari) shows a large decorative cartouche or oval bordered by small floral and geometric elements. A central circular coin ring surrounds a manufacturer's inscription — in Bolognese tradition the ace of coins is the card on which the card-maker places their mark or license seal. The overall effect is ornate and heraldic, the single coin rendered as a cartouche of civic and commercial authority.
sections.Symbol: One coin as sealed cartouche — the manufacturer's mark embedded in the suit's foundational emblem, material value certified by the maker's authority.

sections.Scene: The Fante (Knave) of Coins appears in the double-ended Bolognese format as a jester-like figure in colorful hat and costume, holding coins on each side. The double-ended composition means the figure is visible right-side-up from both directions, a convention that distinguishes Bolognese court cards from the single-direction Marseille courts. The knave holds the suit's circular coin emblem.

sections.Scene: The Ace of Swords (Spade) in the Bolognese deck is among the most ornate of the suit aces. Two large concentric rings decorated in alternating blue and red are flanked by laurel branches, with a central sword or cross shape visible between them and a crown or crest above. The overall composition is heraldic — the ace of swords as a civic or military emblem rather than a simple pip card.
sections.Symbol: One sword as ringed heraldic emblem — the suit's foundational force in its most ornate and ceremonial form, the blade elevated to a civic insignia.

items.name: The Four Papi — the Bolognese Signature

sections.About these cards: The single most distinctive feature of the Bolognese deck: four equal, interchangeable 'Papi' (also called the four Moors) replace the Popess, Empress, Emperor and Pope. They have no fixed order among themselves — whichever is played first outranks the others on that trick. The heretical Popess simply vanishes.
sections.Why it matters: This is exactly what a modern Marseille-named 'Tarocchino' deck gets wrong. The Papi are the test of whether a deck is really Bolognese.
sections.Scene: The four Papi Mori — the Bolognese Moorish substitutes for trumps II through V — are grouped together as the deck's most historically distinctive feature. These four secular figures in colorful court dress replaced the Papessa, Empress, Emperor, and Pope in 18th-century Bologna, a substitution attributed variously to religious caution and to civic regulations on card imagery. Each figure holds a lance and is rendered in the double-ended Bolognese format.
sections.Symbol: The Four Moors as a group — the secular quartet who stand where sacred authority once stood, the card-maker's solution to the problem of representing the Church and Empire in a game.

items.name: The Trumps in the Bolognese Order

sections.About this order: The Bolognese trump order differs from the Marseille: the three virtues (Temperance, Justice, Strength) sit clustered in the middle, and — uniquely — the ANGEL (Judgement) is the HIGHEST trump, ranked ABOVE the World. The deck is one of Dummett's A-order traditions.
sections.How to use: Read the trumps from the Bagattino up to the Angel to see a trump sequence that the printed Marseille never standardised.
sections.Scene: The twenty-two trumps of the Tarocchino Bolognese ordered in the Bolognese sequence: Matto (Fool), Bagatto (I), the four Papi (II-V), Amore (VI), Carro (VII), Temperanza (VIII), Giustizia (VIIII), Forza (X), Ruota (XI), Vecchio (XII), Traditore (XIII), Morte (XIIII), Diavolo (XV), Torre (XVI), Stella (XVII), Luna (XVIII), Sole (XVIIII), Mondo (XX), Angelo (XXI). The Bolognese order places Mondo before Angelo, reversing the standard sequence.
sections.Symbol: The Bolognese trump sequence — the triumphal hierarchy of the tarocchino, distinguished by its four Moors, its Old Man for Hermit, its Traitor for Hanged Man, and its reversal of World and Angel.

sections.About this suit: The Coins (Denari) of the Tarocchino: Ace and 6–10 only, plus the four courts — the Bolognese 'stripping' of the low pips that gives the 62-card deck.
sections.Scene: The suit of Coins (Denari) in the Tarocchino Bolognese includes the Ace plus six through ten (pips two through five having been removed), plus the four court ranks: Fante, Cavallo, Regina, Re. The starburst circular coins of the Italian Denari suit are displayed in clean pip arrangements. The suit's ace carries the manufacturer's cartouche in the Bolognese tradition.
sections.Symbol: The abbreviated coins suit — ten cards rather than fourteen, the suit's material wealth expressed in a truncated sequence that strips out the lower-middle pip range.

sections.About this suit: The Cups (Coppe) of the Tarocchino: Ace and 6–10 only, plus the four courts — the Bolognese 'stripping' of the low pips that gives the 62-card deck.
sections.Scene: The suit of Cups (Coppe) in the Tarocchino Bolognese includes the Ace plus six through ten plus the four courts. The cylindrical Italian chalice forms of the Bolognese coppe are distinctive — taller and more geometric than the goblet shapes of northern European decks. The cups suit's ace bears the suit's heraldic chalice emblem in ornate form.
sections.Symbol: The abbreviated cups suit — ten cards with the lower pips removed, the suit's emotional and receptive domain expressed in the cylindrical Italian chalice.

sections.About this suit: The Swords (Spade) of the Tarocchino: Ace and 6–10 only, plus the four courts — the Bolognese 'stripping' of the low pips that gives the 62-card deck.

The straight-bladed Italian swords of the Bolognese spade are arranged in crossing patterns with decorative shield-nodes at the intersections. The ace bears the suit's distinctive heraldic ring composition.
sections.Symbol: The abbreviated swords suit — ten cards with the lower pips removed, discernment and conflict in the truncated sequence that begins at the mid-range.

sections.Symbol: The abbreviated batons suit — ten cards with the lower pips removed, creative force and enterprise in the truncated Italian pip sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “pip” (24).
sections.Scene: Pip cards in the Tarocchino Bolognese are the numbered non-court suit cards: Ace plus six through ten in each suit (pips two through five having been removed). The pip range is thus: Ace, Six, Seven, Eight, Nine, Ten — six pip cards per suit rather than the ten of a standard tarot deck. This abbreviated pip range is the defining structural feature of the tarocchino game format.
sections.Symbol: Pip — the numbered suit card in its abbreviated Bolognese range, the quantitative ladder that skips the lower-middle rungs.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “bolognese” (22).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all items identified with the Bolognese tradition — the regional Italian tarot variant that developed in Bologna from the 15th century onward, becoming the Tarocchino game. The Bolognese tradition is characterized by the removal of lower pip cards, the substitution of four Moors for the early trumps, the double-ended court format, and a trump ordering distinct from Marseille.
sections.Symbol: Bolognese — the regional tradition that produced the tarocchino, Bologna's answer to the Venetian and Milanese tarot forms it inherited and transformed.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “tarocchino” (22).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all items identified as belonging to the Tarocchino — the specifically Bolognese game format using a 62-card deck (78 cards minus the 2-5 pips from each suit). The tarocchino remained in active play in Bologna into the 20th century, one of the longest-surviving regional tarot game traditions in Europe.
sections.Symbol: Tarocchino — the small tarot (the -ino suffix), Bologna's abbreviated game deck that stripped out the pip cards to accelerate play.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “trionfi” (22).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all trump items identified with the Trionfi tradition — the Italian allegorical sequence of triumphs that forms the conceptual basis of tarot's major arcana. The Bolognese Trionfi follow the sequence of Petrarchan allegorical victories: Love, Chastity, Death, Fame, Time, Eternity — though the specific Bolognese order reflects local adaptations.
sections.Symbol: Trionfi — the triumphal allegory that underlies the tarot's major arcana, the hierarchical parade of victories that orders the trump sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “court” (16).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all court cards in the Tarocchino Bolognese: Fante, Cavallo, Regina, and Re across all four suits. The Bolognese courts are distinctive for their double-ended format and their use of bust or half-figure compositions rather than the full-figure upright presentations of Marseille-tradition decks.
sections.Symbol: Court — the four ranks of human authority in each suit, here expressed in the Bolognese double-ended bust format.

sections.Symbol: Air — the element of thought and discernment, linked to the sword suit's cutting clarity and swift motion.

The aces (Ace of Coins, Cups, Swords, Batons) are the suit-foundation cards, each marked with the manufacturer's emblem in the Bolognese tradition. The aces begin the truncated pip sequence (Ace, Six through Ten) of the tarocchino.
sections.Symbol: Ace — the seed pip, the suit's foundational emblem at the beginning of the Bolognese abbreviated sequence.

sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all four Cavallo (Knight) cards — one per suit — in the Tarocchino Bolognese. The cavalli are the second court rank in the Italian four-court system (Fante/Cavallo/Regina/Re), rendered as double-ended busts in the Bolognese format rather than as mounted horsemen.
sections.Symbol: Cavallo — the knight rank in the Italian court hierarchy, here expressed as a doubled bust rather than as a mounted figure.

sections.Symbol: Eight — the mid-to-upper pip in the Bolognese abbreviated sequence, stability and symmetry in the suit's higher register.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “fante” (4).

Each fante carries the emblem of their suit in a youthful and colorful costume.
sections.Symbol: Fante — the page or knave, the lowest court rank in the Italian hierarchy, the youth who serves as the suit's first court expression.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “nine” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all Nine pip cards across the four suits of the tarocchino. The nines (Nine of Coins, Cups, Swords, Batons) represent the suit approaching its maximum pip count — one step from the ten in the abbreviated Bolognese sequence that runs Ace, Six through Ten.
sections.Symbol: Nine — the near-maximum pip in the Bolognese sequence, the suit approaching but not yet reaching its fullest expression.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “papa / moro” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all four Papa Moro cards — the Bolognese Moorish substitutes for trumps II through V. The term 'Papa Moro' (Moorish Pope) reflects the Bolognese naming of these secular substitute figures who replaced the Papessa, Empress, Emperor, and Pope. Each figure holds a lance and is distinguished by slight variations in costume and headgear.
sections.Symbol: Papa / Moro — the Moorish Pope, the secular substitute who stands where sacred authority once stood, the decorative exotic figure of the Bolognese substitution.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “re” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all four Re (King) cards — one per suit — in the Tarocchino Bolognese. The re are the highest court rank in the Italian four-court system, rendered as double-ended busts in colorful regal costumes. Each king holds or is associated with the emblem of his suit.
sections.Symbol: Re — the king, the highest court rank in the Italian hierarchy, the suit's ultimate authority expressed as a doubled facing bust.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “regina” (4).
sections.Scene: A keyword axis grouping all four Regina (Queen) cards — one per suit — in the Tarocchino Bolognese. The regine are the third court rank in the Italian system (below king, above knight), rendered as double-ended busts in court dress. The inclusion of a queen rather than a second male court rank reflects the Italian four-court system's feminine third rank.
sections.Symbol: Regina — the queen, the third court rank in the Italian hierarchy, the feminine authority who mediates between king and knight in the suit's court sequence.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “seven” (4).

The sevens (Seven of Coins, Cups, Swords, Batons) represent the suit in its active mid-range — in the abbreviated Bolognese sequence the seven sits in the lower-middle of the pip range (Ace, Six, Seven, Eight, Nine, Ten).
sections.Symbol: Seven — the active mid-range pip in the Bolognese abbreviated sequence, the odd number that resists symmetry and suggests the suit in motion.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “six” (4).

sections.Symbol: Six — the first step above the Ace in the Bolognese pip sequence, the lowest numbered pip in the abbreviated tarocchino deck.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “ten” (4).

sections.Symbol: Ten — the highest numbered pip in the Bolognese abbreviated sequence, the suit's maximum pip expression.
